title: South Korea Expands International Alliances with Canada, UK, and EU at Quantum Korea 2026 - Quantum Computing Report
gist: At Quantum Korea 2026, South Korea's Ministry of Science and ICT used the event as a geopolitical platform to sign bilateral quantum frameworks with Canada, the UK, and the EU, aiming to link its sovereign research infrastructure to trusted partners across 16 countries and hedge against tightening deep-tech export controls. Deals include NRF-NSERC joint programs, cross-linking Korea's 2027 Grand Manufacturing Challenge with the UK's GBP 1B ProQure procurement tool, and Horizon Europe / EuroQCI integration.

insights.claim: South Korea's Ministry of Science and ICT used the Quantum Korea 2026 exhibition as a centralized geopolitical cooperation platform to link its sovereign research infrastructure with trusted partners across 16 countries, aiming to mitigate supply-chain vulnerabilities amid tightening global export controls on deep-tech materials.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Quantum is now a supply-chain sovereignty play; nations forming trusted-partner blocs signals a fragmenting, alliance-gated market for hardware and materials.
insights.locator: South Korea's Ministry of Science and ICT

insights.claim: South Korea's bilateral agreement with Canada coordinates resource allocation between the National Research Foundation of Korea and Canada's NSERC, uses Eureka and Eurostars scaling pipelines and Mitacs exchanges, and pairs the Korea Quantum Industry Association with Quantum Industry Canada, drawing collaboration interest from optical hardware developer Xanadu.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Xanadu's named interest flags a concrete commercial channel into the Korean market for photonic quantum players; watch for follow-on B2B deals.

insights.claim: South Korea mapped cross-linkages between its upcoming 2027 Grand Manufacturing Challenge, aimed at securing domestic full-stack quantum computer manufacturing, and the UK's ProQure program, a roughly GBP 1 billion first-buyer public procurement tool managed by Innovate UK to evaluate and scale state-of-the-art quantum prototypes.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: ProQure's GBP 1B first-buyer budget is a demand-side subsidy; hardware vendors should track it as a near-term revenue pool, and Korea's link-in widens their addressable market.

insights.claim: Korea and the UK agreed to co-develop joint physical testbeds, standardize metrics alongside the UK's National Physical Laboratory, and facilitate inter-association sharing between the two countries' quantum industry associations.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Shared benchmarking standards can lock in interoperability advantages; whoever sets the metrics shapes which vendors qualify for procurement.
insights.locator: The two nations agreed to co-develop

insights.claim: South Korea's MSIT finalized execution blueprints with the European Commission's DG CONNECT to integrate Korean hardware infrastructure with Horizon Europe while easing restrictive Technology Readiness Level clauses for foreign deep-tech firms, and to bridge the Korea Quantum Industry Association with the European Quantum Industry Consortium for access to pan-European networking testbeds.
insights.speaker: Mohamed Abdel-Kareem
insights.so_what: Loosening TRL clauses for foreign firms lowers a real barrier to EU funding access; a template other non-EU quantum players may exploit.
